quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gravitational PE (universal)"?

options.text: ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω
The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses
U = -GMm/r; defined as zero at infinite separation; always negative for bound systems
When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gravitational PE (universal)" is defined as: U = -GMm/r; defined as zero at infinite separation; always negative for bound systems. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Total energy in orbit"?

options.text: Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d
E = KE + PE = ½mv² - GMm/r = -GMm/(2r) for circular orbits; negative means bound
x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m

quiz_questions.explanation: "Total energy in orbit" is defined as: E = KE + PE = ½mv² - GMm/r = -GMm/(2r) for circular orbits; negative means bound. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Escape condition"?

options.text: KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
E ≥ 0; when kinetic energy equals or exceeds |PE|, the object can escape to infinity
When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves

quiz_questions.explanation: "Escape condition" is defined as: E ≥ 0; when kinetic energy equals or exceeds |PE|, the object can escape to infinity. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Binding energy"?

options.text: sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM
F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval
Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d
The energy required to completely separate a system; equals |E_total| for a gravitationally bound system

quiz_questions.explanation: "Binding energy" is defined as: The energy required to completely separate a system; equals |E_total| for a gravitationally bound system. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Energy conservation in orbits"?

options.text: v_cm = Σm_i v_i / M_total = p_total / M_total; constant for an isolated system
An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces
The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses

quiz_questions.explanation: "Energy conservation in orbits" is defined as: ½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "U = -GMm/r; defined as zero at infinite separation; always negative for bound systems"?

options.text: Static friction
Dot product
Gravitational PE (universal)
Gravitational field

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Gravitational PE (universal)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E = KE + PE = ½mv² - GMm/r = -GMm/(2r) for circular orbits; negative means bound"?

options.text: Total energy in orbit
Non-conservative work
Gravitational field
Potential energy function

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Total energy in orbit".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E ≥ 0; when kinetic energy equals or exceeds |PE|, the object can escape to infinity"?

options.text: Escape condition
Tension
Angular position (θ)
System of equations

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Escape condition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The energy required to completely separate a system; equals |E_total| for a gravitationally bound system"?

options.text: Common moments of inertia
Linear momentum
Coefficient of restitution
Binding energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Binding energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces"?

options.text: Energy conservation in orbits
Gravitational PE (near Earth)
Torque-angular momentum relation
Kepler's Third Law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Energy conservation in orbits".

An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Gravitational Potential Energy is accurate?

options.text: Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.
When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_i = L_f (I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂).
Circular Motion occurs when an object follows a curved path, requiring a centripetal (center-seeking) acceleration a_c = v²/r directed radially inward.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gravitational Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Gravitational Potential Energy?

options.text: Circular Motion occurs when an object follows a curved path, requiring a centripetal (center-seeking) acceleration a_c = v²/r directed radially inward.
Momentum and Impulse are deeply connected: the impulse (J = ∫F dt) delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (Δp = mΔv).
This formula (with U = 0 at r = ∞) replaces the approximate U = mgh valid only near Earth's surface.
Efficiency η = useful energy output / total energy input ranges from 0 to 1 and is limited by friction, heat losses, and the laws of thermodynamics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gravitational Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Gravitational Potential Energy?

options.text: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and represents the quantity of motion of an object.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.

Energy conservation with U = -GMm/r is essential for orbit analysis: total mechanical energy E = ½mv² - GMm/r determines whether an orbit is bound (E < 0) or unbound (E ≥ 0), and escape velocity follows from setting E = 0.
Conservative Forces and Potential Energy connect force to potential energy through F = -dU/dr (in one dimension) or F = -∇U (in three dimensions).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gravitational Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Gravitational Potential Energy?

options.text: Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
Momentum and Impulse are deeply connected: the impulse (J = ∫F dt) delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (Δp = mΔv).
Friction problems often involve inclined planes, circular motion, and connected bodies.
Dynamics connects force and motion through Newton's three laws.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gravitational Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Gravitational Potential Energy?

options.text: Efficiency η = useful energy output / total energy input ranges from 0 to 1 and is limited by friction, heat losses, and the laws of thermodynamics.
The Work-Energy Theorem (W_net = ΔKE) provides a powerful alternative to Newton's Second Law for solving problems.
This formula (with U = 0 at r = ∞) replaces the approximate U = mgh valid only near Earth's surface.
AP Physics C emphasizes setting up and evaluating these integrals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gravitational Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Gravitational Potential Energy?

options.text: Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles
SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Automotive suspension system analysis modeling shock absorbers as damped SHM to optimize ride comfort and handling characteristics
Calculating the energy required for rocket launches: the difference in gravitational PE between Earth's surface and the desired orbital altitude plus the kinetic energy needed

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gravitational Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Gravitational Potential Energy?

options.text: Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field
Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations
Calculating energy requirements for elevators and conveyor systems using work against gravity W = mgh
Grandfather clock design using the precise period T = 2π√(L/g) of a pendulum to regulate timekeeping mechanisms

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gravitational Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Gravitational Potential Energy applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Analyzing the work required to stretch a bungee cord by integrating the non-linear elastic force over the extension distance
Baseball physics analyzing the impulse of a bat on a ball from high-speed camera force-time data to optimize hitting mechanics
Molecular chemistry modeling interatomic bonds as potential energy curves (Lennard-Jones potential) to predict bond length and bond energy
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gravitational Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Gravitational Potential Energy?

options.text: Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)
Vehicle accident reconstruction using partially inelastic collision models and coefficient of restitution to determine pre-collision speeds
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Calculating the energy required for rocket launches: the difference in gravitational PE between Earth's surface and the desired orbital altitude plus the kinetic energy needed

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gravitational Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Gravitational Potential Energy in practice?

options.text: Tidal force analysis showing how the Moon's gravity varies across Earth's diameter, creating two tidal bulges on opposite sides
Designing roller coasters using calculus-based kinematics to ensure smooth transitions between acceleration phases and safe g-force limits
Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field
Centrifuge design in molecular biology calculating the centripetal acceleration needed to separate cellular components by density

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gravitational Potential Energy.
